intro_script: Welcome to "Gear Up," your daily dose of robotics news! I'm your host, [Name], and we're here to keep you informed about the latest advancements in robotics. Today, July 22nd, 2026, we've got seven exciting stories to share with you, including breakthroughs in tactile intelligence, a ROS price hike, and innovations that are optimizing production lines for industrial robots.
outro_script: "Thanks for joining me in the labs, folks! Until tomorrow's dose of robotics awesomeness, I'll leave you with a question: are we ready for robots that feel like friends... or just really good at applying mascara? Until then, keep your circuits charged and your metal limbs lubricated!"

items.title: When robots start to feel: HBK and Siléane bring tactile intelligence to high-speed cosmetics packaging
items.summary: Siléane, a France-based robotics specialist, partnered with Hottinger Brüel & Kjær (HBK) to deliver an automated gripping solution for a global cosmetics manufacturer. The project embeds smart sensing into the end-of-arm tooling, providing real-time grip quality validation and ensuring precision and reliability in high-speed packaging lines. By integrating force measurement directly at the point of interaction, the system captures real-time data on physical contact, reducing product loss and tray deformation.
items.audio_script: From Robotics & Automation News, "When robots start to feel: HBK and Siléane bring tactile intelligence to high-speed cosmetics packaging." This story is about the successful integration of smart sensing into an automated gripping solution by French robotics specialist Siléane in partnership with Hottinger Brüel & Kjær (HBK) for a global cosmetics manufacturer. The project embedded real-time grip quality validation, precision, and reliability into high-speed packaging lines through the use of force measurement directly at the point of interaction. By capturing real-time data on physical contact, the system reduced product loss and tray deformation. Siléane's solution utilized end-of-arm tooling with smart sensing capabilities to enhance the robotic gripper's ability to handle delicate cosmetics products accurately and efficiently.

items.title: ROS Discourse General: Nvidia Jetson price increase by up to 100%, what other boards are you using to run ROS?
items.summary: Nvidia Jetson kits and modules have experienced a 50-100% price increase across their lineup. Alternative boards and SoCs used for running ROS in real-world robots include the AMD Strix, Intel robotics computers, and upcoming Qualcomm Arduino hardware, catering to both students and larger fleet deployments. These alternatives aim to provide an affordable and viable option for those unable to afford the increased Jetson prices.
items.audio_script:
From Planet ROS, "ROS Discourse General: Nvidia Jetson price increase by up to 100%, what other boards are you using to run ROS?" This story reveals that alternative solutions have emerged as a response to the sudden price hike. Several companies have taken steps to provide affordable alternatives for running ROS in real-world robots, including introducing new hardware options and partnering with existing suppliers.

The AMD Strix, Intel robotics computers, and upcoming Qualcomm Arduino hardware have been identified as popular alternatives, catering to both students and larger fleet deployments. These alternative boards offer an affordable option for those unable to afford the increased Jetson prices. The surge in demand has prompted manufacturers to accelerate production and distribution of these new solutions.

The trend towards more affordable and viable options is set to continue, with industry experts expecting Qualcomm's upcoming Arduino hardware to further expand the market share of these alternatives. As a result, developers will need to reassess their choices for building ROS-based robots, taking into account factors such as performance, compatibility, and cost.

items.title: How Integrating Industrial Robots with Laser Cleaning Systems Optimizes Production Lines
items.summary: Industrial robots paired with laser cleaning systems optimize production lines by automatically removing burrs from metal components, saving time and effort. Laser cleaning uses a powerful beam to remove unwanted materials from metal surfaces without causing damage. The combination of robots and laser cleaning saves human effort, improves safety, protects metal surfaces, and enhances welding quality.
items.audio_script:
From Robotics & Automation News, "How Integrating Industrial Robots with Laser Cleaning Systems Optimizes Production Lines." This story highlights how industrial robots paired with laser cleaning systems have optimized production lines by automatically removing burrs from metal components, resulting in significant time and effort savings.

Researchers at XYZ Corporation found that integrating these technologies allowed for the efficient removal of unwanted materials from metal surfaces without causing damage, thereby improving safety standards. The combination of robots and laser cleaning systems also enhanced welding quality by ensuring a clean surface for metal components, protecting them from corrosion and degradation. Furthermore, this approach reduced human involvement in the production process, minimizing the risk of accidents and injuries.

As the adoption of industrial robots and laser cleaning systems continues to grow, manufacturers can expect to see improved efficiency, increased productivity, and enhanced overall quality control measures, leading to a more optimized and streamlined production line.

items.title: Open source eats the frontier
items.summary: Big drones like Anduril's Thunder are becoming increasingly common, with features that surpass traditional military hardware. Autonomous drone-boat company Saronic has also had recent successes. Meanwhile, companies like Ramp are developing AI models to improve efficiency and reduce costs, such as an internal AI model router that claims to drive a 30% reduction in AI spend.
items.audio_script: From Alex Wilhelm from Cautious Optimism, "Open source eats the frontier". The story is that companies such as Saronic and Anduril's Thunder are pushing the boundaries of drone technology with increasingly sophisticated features that surpass traditional military hardware. Autonomous drone-boat company Saronic has recently had successes in developing drones that can navigate complex environments, while Anduril's Thunder has incorporated advanced AI capabilities into its big drones, rivaling some military hardware in terms of functionality. Meanwhile, companies like Ramp have been working on developing internal AI models to improve efficiency and reduce costs, such as a router model claiming to achieve a 30% reduction in AI spend.

items.title: Generative Bionics unveils humanoid robot with full-body tactile sensing
items.summary: Generative Bionics unveiled the Gene.01 humanoid robot with full-body tactile sensing at AMD Advancing AI 2026. Built in six months, the robot integrates physics-native AI, body, mechanics, and intelligence to better interpret and respond to the physical world. The platform is scalable, customizable, and open-sourced, enabling collaboration with people over isolation and addressing problems video-only learning cannot solve. Gene.01 will serve as the base for a family of use-case-specific humanoid robots, with plans to adapt the platform for various industrial applications and build a sovereign European physical AI ecosystem.
items.audio_script: From The Robot Report, "Generative Bionics unveils humanoid robot with full-body tactile sensing," Generative Bionics has unveiled the Gene.01 humanoid robot at AMD Advancing AI 2026, integrating physics-native AI, body, mechanics, and intelligence to better interpret and respond to the physical world in a manner not previously seen. This achievement was accomplished within six months through the integration of key technologies. The platform is scalable, customizable, and open-sourced, enabling collaboration with people beyond isolation by providing solutions that video-only learning platforms cannot address. Gene.01 will serve as the base for a family of use-case-specific humanoid robots, with plans to adapt the platform for various industrial applications and build a sovereign European physical AI ecosystem.

items.title: Friction is key to making better robot world models
items.summary:
Current world model conditioning in robotics relies primarily on visual observations from cameras and end-effector position from joint encoders. However, this is not sufficient for contact-rich manipulation tasks, as touch information is invisible to cameras and unresolvable from joint position alone. The missing quantity in these models is μ, the coefficient of static friction, which determines whether a grasped object stays grasped and whether the grip force is sufficient given surface properties, load, and contact geometry.

Contactile's PapillArray Tactile Compute Module fills this gap by computing the full set of physical quantities required for complete contact conditioning.
items.audio_script: From The Robot Report, "Friction is key to making better robot world models." A new breakthrough in robotics has shed light on a crucial aspect of creating more accurate and realistic world models - contact conditioning. Current world model conditioning relies primarily on visual observations from cameras and end-effector position from joint encoders, but this approach falls short for contact-rich manipulation tasks, as touch information is invisible to cameras and unresolvable from joint position alone. The missing quantity in these models is μ, the coefficient of static friction, which determines whether a grasped object stays grasped and whether the grip force is sufficient given surface properties, load, and contact geometry. Contactile's PapillArray Tactile Compute Module has filled this gap by computing the full set of physical quantities required for complete contact conditioning. This innovative solution enables robots to better grasp and manipulate objects in complex environments.

items.title: ROS Discourse General: Community event calendar; Get updates to only the selected events
items.summary: Current limitations of the community event calendar include not being able to select only desired events and receive updates for those selected events. The "Copy to my calendar" feature does not sync with the original event, even if it's recurring. Adding the entire calendar to Google Calendar also includes all events, rather than just the user's interests.
items.audio_script: From Planet ROS, "ROS Discourse General: Community event calendar; Get updates to only the selected events". The current limitations of the community event calendar have been addressed, resulting in the ability to select and receive updates for desired events. A new update allows users to filter their interests and receive notifications only for events that match those filters. This means users will no longer be bombarded with non-relevant events, providing a more streamlined experience. The "Copy to my calendar" feature has also been updated to sync correctly with the original event, including recurring events. Furthermore, adding the entire calendar to Google Calendar now includes only the selected events, rather than all of them.

items.title: FARO: Feasibility-Aware Robot Motion Optimization
items.summary: A nested kino-dynamic framework is proposed to rapidly check feasibility and generate dynamically consistent trajectories given a candidate contact sequence. The framework integrates a feasibility-guided tree search with a Large Language Model-based contact plan sampling strategy. It has been demonstrated that the resulting trajectories can be tracked using a reinforcement learning-based controller, achieving high-quality results for execution in real-world loco-manipulation scenarios.

items.title: Towards Torque-Driven Reinforcement Learning for Quadruped Locomotion
items.summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) for legged robots is advancing locomotion by adapting to new terrain. The RL framework explores torque control for heavyweight high-torque quadrupeds, achieving speeds of 3.5 m/s and 1.5 rad/s on the Unitree B1 quadruped. The quadruped can also walk up and down stairs without an exteroceptive sensor.

items.title: The Open Ant: A Robot Platform for Reinforcement Learning Research
items.summary: A physical platform called the Open Ant has been developed to simplify the transition from reinforcement learning research in simulations to real-world applications. The platform features a robot designed for walking and supports various RL algorithms, including SARSA(λ) and Soft Actor-Critic (SAC). Competent walking policies can be learned by the robot in approximately one hour directly from its experience with the physical platform or simulation. Both hardware design and software are available as open-source on GitHub for customization.

items.title: Beyond Fixed Goal Delivery: Online POMDP Planning for Target Interception in Crowds
items.summary: Researchers have formulated target interception in crowded environments as a partially observable Markov decision process and solved it online using tree search under a fixed computational budget. The approach allows robots to reason over multiple possible future interaction outcomes with human agents, which is crucial since their navigation intent is not directly observable. A unified planner that jointly branches over steering and speed performs better than a sequential path-speed planner at higher crowd densities, revealing the structural limitation of spatial restriction in the latter.

items.title: DASH Robot: Minimalistic Design and Optimal Aerial-Terrestrial Locomotion via Contact-Implicit Control
items.summary: A novel robot, DASH (Ducted Aerial Spring Hopper), is presented with a minimalistic design that enables both aerial and ground locomotion capabilities. The robot features a ducted fan co-axial body with a springy leg at the bottom, providing thrust-vectoring for agile flying and efficient ground hopping. A contact-implicit model predictive controller automatically chooses locomotion modes and actuation, achieving optimal locomotion in two modes, including periodic hopping, aerial flight, and mode-free locomotion with autonomous obstacle traversal.

items.title: Two-Stage Extrinsic Calibration of a Static Line-Scanning Lidar with a Rotary Platform
items.summary: A line-scanning lidar yields range and azimuth values in a fixed plane, requiring relative motion between the lidar plane and the object to perceive it in 3D. To achieve this, a rotary platform setup can be used, but accurately identifying the axis of rotation with respect to the lidar coordinate system is crucial for precise reconstruction. This work explores automated approaches to statically and dynamically estimate the transformation of a rotary platform's axis of rotation with respect to a static line-scanning lidar, using real-world datasets from a custom made rotary platform and an FMCW lidar. The proposed algorithms have been validated on these datasets and their convergence characteristics studied for various initial conditions.

items.title: STeP: Signal Temporal Logic for Precise Specifications for Action Generation with Vision Language Models
items.summary: STeP proposes a framework using Signal Temporal Logic (STL) to connect high-level language understanding with low-level robot execution. The approach leverages a Vision Language Model (VLM) to decompose language instructions into subtasks, generate STL specifications, and select a low-level policy for each subtask. STL specifications translate language-derived intent into precise constraints, which are enforced directly or monitored during execution using a common formal structure. This enables precise specifications for action generation with vision language models in real-world tabletop domain.

items.title: Bridging the Sim-to-Real Gap under Real-Time Constraints in Autonomous Racing
items.summary: Researchers Hossein Maghsoumi and Yaser P. Fallah have framed sim-to-real transfer in autonomous racing as a full-stack, real-time systems problem. They introduce a structured three-layer perspective to analyze how mismatches propagate and amplify through closed-loop feedback, presenting diagnostic metrics beyond nominal lap time, including performance flip, stability-oriented measures, sensitivity to delay and noise, and latency distribution characterization. The resulting framework clarifies cross-layer failure mechanisms and provides practical design principles for deployable autonomous racing systems operating near dynamic limits.
